author: Zhifeng Chen
podcast_script:
<Person1>Today we’re digging into a piece of infrastructure from Uber Engineering: uForwarder, which is their open-source “consumer proxy” for Kafka that enables async queuing patterns. If you’ve ever tried to use Kafka like a work queue—where you want lots of independent consumers, strong isolation, retries, delays, and you don’t want every team reinventing consumer group management—this is right in that wheelhouse.</Person1>

<Person2>And Uber isn’t doing this at small scale. They’re processing trillions of messages and multiple petabytes a day. So whatever they build has to survive the real world: failures, poison pills, uneven traffic, multi-region deployments, and, honestly, the economics of running massive fleets.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. The blog is essentially: “We built this consumer proxy three years ago, it became the default way to read Kafka for pub-sub use cases at Uber, we onboarded over a thousand consumer services—and then we hit production challenges.” And the post is about the key problems they ran into and the specific solutions they implemented: hardware efficiency, consumer isolation, head-of-line blocking, and delay processing.</Person1>

<Person2>Let’s start with the core idea. What is a “consumer proxy” in this context? Because Kafka already has consumers.</Person2>

<Person1>Kafka does. But Kafka’s native consumption model pushes a lot of complexity to the application: consumer groups, partition assignment, rebalancing behavior, offset commits, backpressure, parallelism per partition, handling poison-pill messages, and also language-specific client behavior.

Uber’s consumer proxy flips the model. Instead of each service running its own Kafka consumer group, there’s a proxy cluster that speaks Kafka’s binary protocol to fetch messages. Then it pushes messages to consumer service instances over gRPC. The consumer service exposes a gRPC endpoint—basically “here’s a message, process it”—and returns a gRPC status code. The proxy aggregates results and commits offsets to Kafka only when it’s safe.</Person1>

<Person2>So the consumer services become stateless handlers, and the proxy becomes the stateful Kafka-aware component.</Person2>

<Person1>That’s the key. It abstracts Kafka away. For a service owner, you implement a gRPC handler rather than embedding Kafka client logic. That reduces misconfigurations and standardizes behavior, and it also helps with what they call the “impedance mismatch” between Kafka partitions and message queuing use cases.</Person1>

<Person2>Let’s unpack that mismatch. Kafka partitions are great for ordered logs, but queues often want different semantics: lots of workers, dynamic scaling, sometimes out-of-order processing, and not being constrained by partition counts.</Person2>

<Person1>Right. With Kafka, your maximum parallelism in a consumer group is bounded by partition count. If you have 50 partitions, you can’t effectively have 500 fully utilized consumers in one group. You can increase partitions, but that has operational and data implications, and it’s not always feasible—especially if the topic is used for multiple downstream purposes like analytics, ingestion, streaming jobs, and so on.</Person1>

<Person2>So the proxy is acting like a layer that can fan out messages to many service instances without forcing the Kafka topic to have huge partition counts.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes, and it can also implement queue-like features. But once you do that, you inherit a new set of failure modes and scaling problems. The blog’s “Emerging Challenges” section lists four big ones: head-of-line blocking, hardware efficiency, delay processing, and isolation.</Person1>

<Person2>Let’s go one by one. Head-of-line blocking is a classic. In Kafka, one bad message can prevent offset commits and stall progress for a partition. What was special here?</Person2>

<Person1>Uber had already introduced out-of-order commits to improve resiliency. That’s interesting: instead of requiring strict sequential completion, they track which offsets are done and commit when contiguous ranges are complete, allowing parallel processing.

They also had a dead-letter queue (DLQ) concept for poison pills—messages that will never succeed. But they discovered a specific class of “poison pill” that their previous approach couldn’t mitigate: fail-in-transit messages.</Person1>

<Person2>Fail-in-transit meaning the message never even reaches the consumer handler, so the handler can’t return a failure status that would trigger DLQ logic?</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. They list a few causes:

1) gRPC payload size limits. Default gRPC server max request size is 4MB. If a Kafka message is bigger, the request can be rejected before the handler sees it.

2) Invalid consumer instances under isolation routing. If certain messages must go to certain instances and those instances are unhealthy, you can’t deliver those messages.

3) Request filtering via interceptors. A service might have interceptors that reject requests before handler logic.

In all of those cases, you can end up with a message at the head of the partition that can’t be processed, and because of offset commit rules, it can block everything behind it.</Person1>

<Person2>So even with out-of-order commits, if the tracker fills up and the head offset can’t be resolved, you’re stuck.</Person2>

<Person1>Right, and they call this the “most severe problem”: a completely stuck consumer.

Their solution is “Active Head-of-Line Blocking Resolution,” which has two parts: detection and mitigation.</Person1>

<Person2>How do they detect it?</Person2>

<Person1>They use their out-of-order commit tracker. Think of it as a per-partition structure that tracks the status of offsets in flight: processed, unprocessed, maybe failed, etc.

They look at two signals:

- Tracker utilization: how full the tracker is. If the tracker still has empty slots, you’re not blocked yet.

- Percentage of uncommitted messages: this is subtle. Head-of-line blocking is “a small number of messages blocking a large number of messages.” So if the tracker is very full but only a tiny fraction is uncommitted, it suggests that almost everything is done except a few offsets—likely near the head—that are preventing commits from advancing.

They set a detection condition:

- Utilization > UTILIZATION_THRESHOLD
- Uncommitted percentage < UNCOMMITTED_PERCENTAGE_THRESHOLD

In practice: 90% utilization and 2% uncommitted.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s clever. It’s basically: “we’re almost saturated, and we’re waiting on a tiny set of offsets.” That’s the signature of head-of-line blocking.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. Then mitigation: the goal is to commit past the stuck point without data loss.

Their mitigation procedure is:

1) Mark the head offset as CANCELED in the tracker.
2) Cancel gRPC requests, including ongoing or future retries.
3) Send the message to the dead-letter queue.
4) Mark the offset as COMMITTED.

So they force forward progress by explicitly DLQ’ing the problematic message and committing it, after canceling any in-flight attempts.</Person1>

<Person2>So it’s “active” because the system detects the stall and takes action, rather than waiting for someone to notice consumer lag and manually intervene.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes, it’s self-healing. And at Uber scale, manual intervention doesn’t scale.

Now, the second big challenge: hardware efficiency. When you centralize consumption into a proxy fleet, that fleet can grow huge. They started with tens of servers, then hundreds, then thousands. Costs balloon, and utilization becomes critical.</Person1>

<Person2>And also: the proxy is doing a lot. Fetching, buffering, dispatching over gRPC, tracking offsets, retries, and maybe doing delay logic. So you need to place workloads efficiently across workers.</Person2>

<Person1>That leads into their “Consumer Auto Rebalancer.” It’s important to distinguish this from Kafka’s native rebalance. Their proxy makes consumer services stateless and offloads the rebalance process from the service, but the system still needs to assign partitions—or more generally, “workloads”—to proxy workers.

They frame it as two problems:

- Workload sizing: how much CPU, memory, network does each workload require?
- Workload placement: how do you pack workloads of varying sizes onto worker instances of fixed capacity?</Person1>

<Person2>What’s a “workload” here? Is it like a topic-partition, or a consumer group, or something else?</Person2>

<Person1>From the description, it’s basically the unit of assignment the proxy controller moves around—likely tied to consumption responsibilities for a given topic/partition set and consumer destination. The key is that it has measurable traffic: request rate, concurrency, bytes rate.

For sizing, they use an adaptive approach: observe traffic metrics and compute required hardware resources. Then continuously run the procedure to converge computed scale and actual scale.</Person1>

<Person2>And they do different time windows for scale up vs scale down: fast scale-up, slow scale-down.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. That’s a classic control systems trick. Fast scale-up minimizes consumer lag when you suddenly need more capacity. Slow scale-down avoids thrashing and also minimizes message duplication caused by “workload placement shuffle.”

That’s an important point: when you move a workload between workers, you can cause retries, replays, or duplicates depending on where you are in processing and what’s committed. So you want stability.</Person1>

<Person2>Then placement: how do they avoid constantly moving workloads around?</Person2>

<Person1>They make assignments “sticky.” Once a workload is assigned to a worker, it doesn’t change unless there’s a placement violation. Violations include:

- Workload size violation: it grew beyond the worker’s capacity.
- Worker liveness failure.

And they pack multiple workloads of different sizes onto the same worker as long as the total fits within capacity.</Person1>

<Person2>So it’s like bin packing with constraints, but with stickiness to reduce churn.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. The outcome: better hardware utilization without overloading workers.

Now the third challenge: delay processing. This is basically “don’t process this message until some time has passed,” which comes up when downstream dependencies aren’t ready, or you want scheduled retries without building a separate system.</Person1>

<Person2>Many teams implement this badly: consumer receives message, checks a database or external API, sees it’s not ready, then sleeps or retries, or they produce to a delay queue, or they build a cron-like scheduler. It spreads complexity everywhere.</Person2>

<Person1>Uber saw that too. They wanted built-in delay processing in the proxy to simplify service workflows and reduce operational overhead.

They had an earlier delay mechanism for retry topics: for each message, the fetcher thread checks whether the delay elapsed; if not, it pauses until it does. But that pauses the entire fetcher thread, meaning it can’t process other messages. To avoid blocking and wasting compute, they restricted retry topics to a single partition.</Person1>

<Person2>Which is a scalability killer, because Kafka’s whole point is partitioning.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. And you can’t apply “single partition only” to main topics.

So they introduced a DelayProcessManager inside the fetcher thread, with a partition-level pause/resume approach.

It does three main things:

1) Verify delay: for the first unprocessed message in a partition, check if the required delay is satisfied.

2) Pause partitions: if not satisfied, pause that specific topic partition using KafkaConsumer.pause(). Store the polled but unprocessed messages in an in-memory buffer. This avoids repeatedly polling the same messages and wasting resources.

3) Resume partitions: before polling new messages, resume any partitions whose delay has now elapsed using KafkaConsumer.resume(). Merge buffered messages with newly polled messages for processing.</Person1>

<Person2>So instead of pausing the entire fetch loop, you only pause partitions that have “not ready yet” messages at their head, and you keep consuming from other partitions in the same thread.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. That maintains scalability and throughput.

There’s a nuance they call out: it guarantees an “at-least delay,” not an exact delay. Because partitions are resumed only after the worker finishes processing the current batch. If processing takes longer than the delay, the effective delay is longer. But for most delay use cases, “not before” is what matters.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s a very practical tradeoff. Exact scheduling would require a more complex timing wheel or separate scheduler threads, and you’d still be at the mercy of load.</Person2>

<Person1>Right.

Now the fourth challenge: isolation. This is where things get very real in large organizations. By default, the proxy pushes messages to a consumer service through a network load balancer that spreads traffic across instances. That means all instances share all messages.

But Uber wanted message isolation along dimensions like:

- Production vs non-production isolation: keep non-prod traffic from polluting prod services or data.

- Zone isolation: keep messages produced in a zone routed to consumers in the same zone to reduce blast radius if a zone fails.</Person1>

<Person2>And the naive solution they mention is to split topics—create child topics per subset—and have each consumer instance read its own topic. But that breaks compatibility because topics are shared across many downstream uses beyond just messaging.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. Topic proliferation also creates operational overhead and can ripple into analytics and ingestion pipelines.

Their solution is context-aware routing. The producer injects “subsetting context” into the Kafka message headers. The consumer proxy converts that context into gRPC request headers. Then the load balancer performs context-aware routing based on those headers.</Person1>

<Person2>So routing decisions are made at the load balancer layer, using metadata that originated in Kafka headers.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. And that allows them to implement zone isolation and prod/non-prod isolation without changing the topic structure.

It’s a good example of using Kafka headers as a control plane for delivery semantics, while keeping payload and topic stable.</Person1>

<Person2>It also implies you need consistent propagation of headers: producer must set them, proxy must map them correctly, and the load balancer must understand them.</Person2>

<Person1>Right. It’s an end-to-end contract.

Now, stepping back: uForwarder is the open-source name for this consumer proxy. Uber says it became the primary option for reading Kafka in pub-sub use cases, with over 1,000 consumer services onboarded. That’s a huge internal adoption signal.</Person1>

<Person2>And they open-sourced it after companies reached out following their earlier blog post. That’s interesting: it suggests these pain points aren’t unique to Uber—Kafka-as-queue problems show up everywhere.</Person2>

<Person1>Definitely. Many organizations end up building some form of “Kafka consumer abstraction layer” because the raw client model is powerful but easy to misuse.

Let’s talk a bit about why pushing messages over gRPC is such a big deal. In Kafka, consumers pull. Here, the proxy pulls from Kafka but pushes to services. That changes backpressure and concurrency control.</Person1>

<Person2>How does the proxy know when it’s safe to commit offsets if it’s pushing messages individually?</Person2>

<Person1>That’s where the aggregation and tracking comes in. The proxy receives per-message processing results—gRPC status codes—from consumer instances. It aggregates them and commits “proper offsets” when safe.

Because they support parallel processing, they need out-of-order commit tracking: you can process offset 105 before 101 finishes, but you can’t commit 105 if 101 isn’t resolved unless you have logic to track contiguous committed ranges and handle gaps. The tracker is the structure that supports that.</Person1>

<Person2>And that same tracker becomes a sensor for detecting head-of-line blocking. Nice reuse.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. Observability plus control.

Now, the blog also hints at future work. Two notable “next steps”:

1) Handling consumer lag by rewinding offsets to latest for freshness while spinning up a side consumer to catch up lagging data. That’s like a dual-track consumption model: one path prioritizes fresh events for real-time needs, another backfills for completeness.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s a tricky tradeoff. Many real-time systems prefer “freshness over completeness” under extreme lag, but you don’t want to lose data; you want to catch up eventually.</Person2>

<Person1>Right.

2) Protobuf support. Since Uber uses gRPC/Protobuf heavily internally, stakeholders want to receive Protobuf objects directly instead of raw Kafka bytes that they must decode themselves. So they’re building deeper schema-aware integration.</Person1>

<Person2>That also suggests uForwarder today is payload-agnostic: it forwards bytes and relies on consumers to decode. Protobuf support could standardize decoding and reduce duplication, but it introduces schema management concerns.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly—schema evolution, compatibility, and how you choose which schema to decode with. But it’s a natural request when gRPC is already the transport.

Before we wrap, let’s connect the dots: what’s the “key insight” from this blog beyond “we built a proxy”?</Person1>

<Person2>For me, it’s that once you centralize Kafka consumption into a shared proxy, you can implement platform-level guarantees and features that are hard to do consistently across hundreds of teams: isolation routing, automatic mitigation of head-of-line blocking, standardized retries and DLQ behavior, and built-in delay processing.</Person2>

<Person1>And you can do it while improving cost efficiency through centralized autoscaling and workload placement. But you also have to treat the proxy itself as a large distributed system: it needs its own rebalancer, its own failure handling, and careful control of resource utilization.

The blog is very concrete: it gives thresholds for HOL detection, describes the mitigation steps, explains the partition pause/resume approach for delay, and describes stickiness and adaptive sizing for efficient placement.</Person1>

<Person2>If someone listening wanted to apply this idea in their own organization, what would you say are the prerequisites?</Person2>

<Person1>First, you need a strong reason to abstract Kafka consumption—like many consumer services, multiple languages, frequent misconfigurations, or a desire for queue-like semantics.

Second, you need operational maturity: the proxy becomes critical infrastructure. You’ll need metrics, alerting, safe rollout, and clear contracts for delivery semantics, retries, DLQ, and idempotency.

Third, you need to think about isolation and routing early. Uber’s context-aware routing is elegant, but it requires producers to set headers and infrastructure to route on them.

And finally, you need a plan for poison pills and fail-in-transit cases. The active HOL resolution is a good pattern: detect automatically, cancel in-flight work, DLQ, and move on.</Person1>

<Person2>Also, at-least-once delivery is still the likely outcome. If you’re committing offsets based on acknowledgments, duplicates can happen, especially during rebalances and failures. So consumers need idempotency or de-duplication strategies.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes, and Uber alludes to message duplication risk during placement shuffles—another sign they’re operating in at-least-once territory.

So that’s uForwarder: an open-source consumer proxy that makes Kafka act more like an async queue for services, with production-grade solutions for isolation, delay, head-of-line blocking, and cost-efficient scaling.</Person1>

<Person2>And it’s a great reminder that “just use Kafka” is rarely the full story at large scale. The platform layer matters, and sometimes the best move is to build a shared abstraction that encodes the organization’s hard-won operational lessons.</Person2>

<Person1>Well said. If you want to explore it, Uber has open-sourced uForwarder on GitHub, and the blog walks through the design choices and the evolution from initial prototype to production-hardened system.</Person1>

title: How Uber Scaled Data Replication to Move Petabytes Every Day | Uber Blog
summary: Uber’s HiveSync service relies on Hadoop DistCp to replicate a 350+ PB data lake across on‑prem and cloud regions for reliable access and disaster recovery, but rapid growth to ~1 PB/day and hundreds of thousands of daily jobs exposed DistCp bottlenecks in job planning and YARN overhead. Uber customized DistCp by moving Copy Listing/Input Splitting into the YARN Application Master, parallelizing both Copy Listing and Copy Committer, and using “Uber jobs” to run single-mapper copies inside the AM to avoid container startup costs. The result was ~90% lower job submission latency, major reductions in listing and commit tail latencies, elimination of ~268k container launches/day, a 5× increase in incremental replication capacity, and successful bulk migration of 306 PB to the cloud with improved observability and operational controls.

# Introduction

Uber prioritizes a reliable data lake, which is distributed across on-premise and cloud environments. This multi-region setup presents challenges for ensuring reliable and timely data access due to limited network bandwidth and the need for seamless data availability, particularly for disaster recovery. Uber uses the [Hive Sync service](https://www.uber.com/en-IN/blog/building-ubers-data-lake-batch-data-replication-using-hivesync/), which uses Apache Hadoop® Distcp (Distributed Copy) for data replication. However, with Uber’s Data Lake exceeding 350 PB, Distcp’s limitations became apparent. This blog explores the optimizations made to Distcp to enhance its performance and meet Uber’s growing data replication and disaster recovery needs across its distributed infrastructure.

## Understanding Distcp

Distcp is an open-source framework for copying large datasets between different locations in a distributed manner. It uses Hadoop’s MapReduce framework to parallelize and distribute the copy tasks across multiple nodes, allowing for faster and more scalable data transfers, particularly in large-scale environments.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490376086-1024x640.png)

Figure 1: High-level Distcp architecture.

---

### Distcp Architecture

The Distcp architecture comprises several key components:

- **Distcp Tool:** Identifies files, groups them into blocks (Copy Listing), defines distribution across mappers, and submits the configured Hadoop job to YARN.
- **Hadoop Client:** Sets up the job environment, determines which mappers handle specific blocks (Input Splitting), and submits the job to YARN.
- **RM (Resource Manager):** The YARN component that schedules tasks, receives the Distcp job, allocates resources, and delegates execution to the Application Master.
- **AM (Application Master):** Monitors the MapReduce job life cycle, requests resources (containers) from the RM for Copy Mapper tasks, and consolidates file splits at the destination.
- **Copy Mapper:** Performs the actual data copying of assigned file blocks, running in a container managed by the YARN Node Manager.
- **Copy Committer:** Merges the copied blocks to assemble the final files in the destination file system.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490375966-806x1024.png)

Figure 2: Diagram showing Distcp from the */src/* directory to the */dest/* directory.

---

Figure 2 shows how Distcp replicates three files from the source directory */src/* to the destination directory */dest/* using the components described earlier. The source directory contains three files—File 1, File 2, and File 3—all of the same size. The Copy Listing task, running on the client side, identifies these files and splits each into two blocks (chunks). The Input Splitting task then distributes these file blocks across three mappers.

- **Map 1** receives one block from File 1 and another from File 2.
- **Map 2** handles one block from File 3 and one from File 1.
- **Map 3** processes one block from File 2 and another from File 3.

The Copy Mapper tasks then copy these blocks from the source to the destination directory. After replication, the Copy Committer task runs in the AM, merging the corresponding blocks of each file to recreate the final three files in the destination directory.

---

## How HiveSync Uses Distcp

HiveSync was initially built on the open-source Airbnb® [ReAir](https://github.com/airbnb/reair) project. It supports both bulk (allowing large amounts of data copy in one pass) and incremental replication (syncing incremental updates as new data arrives), keeping Uber’s data lakes in sync across both HDFS™ (Hadoop Distributed File System) and cloud-based object storage. It uses Distcp for large-scale data replication.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490389357-990x1024.png)

Figure 3: HiveSync architecture: Data replication workflow using Distcp.

---

Figure 3 shows how Hivesync server listens for copy requests from the source Hive cluster. For sizes larger than 256 MB, it submits Distcp jobs to an executor. Multiple workers (asynchronous threads) then prepare and submit these jobs in parallel to YARN using the Hadoop client. A monitoring thread tracks the progress of each job, and once the job is completed successfully, the data is available in the destination cluster.

---

## Scaling Issues with HiveSync: The Breaking Point

By Q3 2022, HiveSync faced significant scaling challenges as the daily data replication volume surged from 250 TB to 1 PB in just one quarter.

One factor contributing to this rapid increase was the concentration of data writes in a single data center. In 2022, Uber shifted to an active-passive data lake architecture for cost savings, moving from evenly distributed data generation to having the primary on-premise data center handle 90% of data generation and most batch-compute tasks. This significantly increased the load on the HiveSync server replicating data from the primary to the secondary region. The SRC (Single Region Compute) project’s impact will be discussed separately.

Another factor was onboarding all on-premise Hive datasets to HiveSync. With the new active-passive model, HiveSync became essential for disaster recovery, ensuring data generated in one region was replicated in another geographic region. This required scaling HiveSync to cover Uber’s entire data lake. In just one quarter, the number of datasets managed by HiveSync grew from 30,000 to 144,000, as new datasets were onboarded. This more than doubled the number of replication requests.

As a result of these, the number of daily replication jobs skyrocketed from 10,000 to an average of 374,000, far outpacing the system’s ability to process them. This led to a substantial backlog, making it increasingly difficult to meet the promised SLAs for replication delays. Specifically, the P100 replication lag SLA of 4 hours and the P99.9 SLO of 20 minutes became challenging to uphold at this new scale.

Additionally, the scale of replication requests was expected to increase significantly as HiveSync took on a crucial role in migrating Uber’s data lake [from on-premises to cloud regions](https://www.uber.com/en-IN/blog/modernizing-ubers-data-infrastructure-with-gcp/?uclick_id=f08b5468-ea9f-4f1f-a440-357109448b6e). This shift was predicted to nearly double the scale and volume of copy requests, putting more pressure on HiveSync to manage the increased workload and optimize its data replication processes to meet the challenges of operating in a cloud infrastructure.

---

## Key Improvements

We’ve introduced the following enhancements to Distcp to customize it for our scaling demands. These optimizations dramatically improved Uber’s data replication scale and efficiency.

This includes

- Shifting the resource-intensive Copy Listing and Input Splitting tasks to the Application Master, which reduced job submission latency by 90% by mitigating HDFS client contention.
- Parallelizing Copy Listing and Copy Committer tasks that significantly cut down job planning and completion times.
- Implementing *Uber jobs* for small transfers that helped in eliminating 268,000 container launches daily, optimizing YARN resource usage.

Let’s take a closer look at each of them in detail –

### Shifting Distcp Preparation Task to AM

During one of the incidents, we noticed under heavy load on the system, an increase in file system latency led to a corresponding rise in Distcp copy-listing latency.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490376049-1024x791.png)

Figure 4: Increased HDFS FSUtils latency directly affects Distcp *Copy Listing* task.

---

When we analyzed the thread dumps during the spike, we found that most threads were stuck waiting for a lock held by the HDFS client for Remote Procedure Calls. This approach doesn’t scale well in a highly multithreaded environment.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490376845-1024x695.png)

Figure 5: Threads blocked on RPC calls.

---

In a typical Distcp submission flow, several components rely on the HDFS client: the Distcp worker for data comparisons, the Distcp tool for Copy Listing, and the Hadoop Client for Input Splitting. As the number of Distcp executor threads increases, so does the number of parallel HDFS client users.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490375027-871x1024.png)

Figure 6: Multiple parallel calls from different copy job requests create contention on the HDFS client.

---

We found that while Distcp scales data copying well, it also handles file planning and listing tasks on the client side. This preparation phase, where files to be copied are identified (Input Splitting), caused bottlenecks because it relied on a shared HDFS client, which was also used by other HiveSync components. As data volumes and the number of Distcp workers grew, JVM-level locks in the HDFS client became a major issue, leading to thread contention as parallelism increased. This caused delays, with Copy Listing alone responsible for 90% of the job submission latency.

The problem was made worse by the large number of NameNode calls, which are proportional to the number of files being copied—particularly problematic with large directories.

To ease the load on a single HDFS client, we shifted the resource-heavy tasks of Copy Listing and Input Splitting from the HiveSync server to the AM.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490372570-edited.png)

Figure 7: Moved Copy Listing and Input Splitting processes from Hive Sync server (Client) to AM.

---

Now, each Distcp job performs Copy Listing on its own AM container, which significantly reduces lock contention on the HiveSync Hadoop client. It helped us achieve a 90% reduction in Distcp job submission latency.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490404372-1024x603.png)

Figure 8: Observed 90% reduction in Distcp job submission time.

---

### Parallelizing Copy Listing Task

DistCp tool runs the Copy Listing task to generate filesystem blocks for the files to be copied. These blocks are written into a sequence file, forming a list of file chunks to be copied from the source to the destination cluster by the Copy Mapper task. During this process, the main thread sequentially calls the namenode using the [getFileBlockLocations API](https://hadoop.apache.org/docs/stable/api/org/apache/hadoop/fs/FileSystem.html#getFileBlockLocations-org.apache.hadoop.fs.FileStatus-long-long-) for files larger than a specified chunk size to create file splits (chunks). It also retries when file status checks fail, making this the most resource-intensive part of DistCp.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490375821-1024x483.png)

Figure 9: p99 latency averaged at around 10 mins for the busiest replication server even after moving this task to Application Master.

---

We observed that multiple files can be listed in parallel and written in any order in the sequence file. However, the chunks of each file need to stay together and in order, because the Copy Committer algorithm uses them to merge the copied file splits at the destination. Building on this idea, we parallelized the file system namenode calls to reduce Copy Listing latency by assigning a separate thread to create splits for each file, adding them to a blocking queue, with a separate writer thread sequentially writing the blocks to the sequence file. This approach helped improve the Distcp job completion time.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490375237.png)

Figure 10: Copy Listing task V2 workflow.

---

In Figure 10, the listing function uses multithreading to retrieve files from the source cluster via namenode calls. Each thread handles the creation of blocks for one file, allowing parallel processing of multiple files. For example,*/src/file1* (1684 MB) is split into two chunks: the first chunk (*/src/file1/part0*) has 4 HDFS blocks of 256 MB each, and the second chunk (*/src/file1/part1*) has 3 blocks (2 of 256 MB and 1 of 128 MB). The listing thread synchronously adds these chunks to a blocking queue, while a separate writer thread regularly polls the queue and writes both chunks to the sequence file in order. For fast failure handling, if any thread fails, the main thread stops the process and retries the Distcp job. After the listing function completes and all items in the queue are written to the sequence file, it updates the job status using the Status Updater.

We achieved a 60% reduction in the p99 average Distcp listing latency and a 75% decrease in the maximum latency across all HiveSync servers by using 6 threads.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490405298-1024x503.png)

Figure 11: Improvement in Copy Listing latency on a Hive Sync server with 6 threads.

---

### Parallelizing Copy Committer task

After the Distcp Copy Mapper tasks complete copying file splits from the source to the destination directory, the Copy Committer Task in the AM merges these splits into full files. For directories with over 500,000 files, this process can take up to 30 minutes. The open-source version merges file chunks sequentially, leading to slower performance.

To address this, we parallelized the file concatenation process, with each thread responsible for merging one file at a time. The Sequence file created during the Copy Listing process is used to identify the order of blocks of individual files that need to be merged at the destination.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490383440-940x1024.png)

Figure 12: Copy Committer task V2 workflow.

---

In Figure 12, mappers retrieve the file splits created during the Copy Listing process from the Sequence File and copy them to the destination directory under */dest/*. Each concatenation thread (Concatenator) gathers the splits for a specific file and merges them to create the final file. The three splits of file 1 (*/dest/file\_part0**,* */dest/file\_part1*, and */dest/file\_part2*) are combined to form */dest/file1* at the destination. Similarly for file 2 and file 3. For quick failure handling, if any thread encounters an issue, the main thread halts the process and retries the Distcp job

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490375203-1024x433.png)

Figure 13: Mean concat latency dropped by 97.29% using 10 threads.

---

### Uberize Single Mapper Jobs:  Improve YARN Usage

Around 52% of Distcp jobs submitted by HiveSync servers require only one mapper to copy less than 512 MB and fewer than 200 files. Although these small jobs finish quickly, a significant portion of time is spent on environment setup (allocating a new container in yarn and JVM startup time) rather than copying itself.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490391599-1024x493.png)

Figure 14: More than 50% of Distcp jobs are assigned a single mapper each.

---

To address this overhead, we leveraged Hadoop’s “Uber jobs” feature, which eliminates the need to allocate and run tasks in separate containers. Instead, the Copy Mapper tasks are executed directly within the Application Master’s JVM, reducing unnecessary container allocation.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490375551-1024x1014.png)

Figure 15: Uber Job workflow.

---

In Figure 15, the AM determines whether a job qualifies as an Uber job. If it does, the Copy Mapper task is executed locally within the AM’s JVM. Otherwise, the AM requests a container via the Node Manager and runs the Copy Mapper task there. Once the task is complete, the AM initiates the Copy Committer task to merge file splits at the destination.

We enabled Uber jobs with the following configurations:

- *mapreduce.job.ubertask.enable: true*
- *mapreduce.job.ubertask.maxmaps: 1* (ensures only 1 mapper is used)
- *mapreduce.job.ubertask.maxbytes: 512 MB* (limits data copy to 512 MB)

By implementing this approach, we eliminated the need for around 268,000 single-core container launches per day, significantly improving YARN resource usage and job efficiency.

---

## Impact

### Significant 5x Boost in Incremental Data Replication Capacity

The improvements we made to the Distcp tool at Uber have greatly boosted our incremental data replication abilities across both on-premise and cloud data centers. Thanks to these changes, we’ve increased our data handling capacity across on-premises by 5 times in just one year, without running into any incidents related to scaling.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490377430-1024x669.png)

Figure 16: Scale of Hivesync across on-premise and cloud data centers.

---

### Seamless Bulk Data Migration from On-Premise to Cloud

In recent months, we’ve expanded Hivesync’s capabilities to support the replication of on-premise data lake to a cloud-based data lake, as highlighted [here](https://www.uber.com/en-IN/blog/modernizing-ubers-data-infrastructure-with-gcp/?uclick_id=f08b5468-ea9f-4f1f-a440-357109448b6e). Enhancements to Distcp have played a crucial role in handling the scale of this migration. To date, we’ve successfully migrated over 306 PB of data to the cloud.

---

![Image](https://blog.uber-cdn.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=2160,quality=80,onerror=redirect,format=auto/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image-17696490374135-1024x375.png)

Figure 17: Data migrated from on-premise to cloud via the Hivesync service.

---

### Better Observability

We introduced several key metrics that significantly improved observability. These metrics provided insights into Distcp job submission times on both the client and Yarn AM sides, job submission rates, and the performance of key Distcp components like Copy Listing and Copy Committer tasks. We also tracked metrics such as maximum heap memory usage of Hadoop containers, p99 Distcp copy rate per job, and overall copy rate. This enhanced visibility allowed us to better monitor and understand the replication rate of our service, and it played a crucial role in mitigating and diagnosing multiple incidents.

---

## Challenges

We faced several challenges during the rollout of the changes to production servers. One challenge was OOM (out of memory) exceptions in AM. Rigorous stress testing helped in identifying optimal memory and core usage. We added metrics to detect OOM issues, which helped us later in identifying optimal YARN resource configurations for memory-intensive copy requests.

Another issue was a high job submission rate from HiveSync. Lowering submission latency increased the job submission rate, which often led to “Yarn Queue Full” errors. To prevent overloading YARN, we implemented a circuit breaker in HiveSync that temporarily pauses new submissions until retries are successful. We added metrics to detect such events, enabling real-time monitoring and adjustments to Yarn queue capacity as needed. Managing high copy rates, though efficient, caused high network bandwidth usage and required careful tuning to balance performance with resource limits.

We also faced AM failures due to a long-running Copy Listing task. Initially, the Copy Listing and Input Splitting parts were moved to the setup phase of the AM. This caused problems because the RM expects the AM to send regular heartbeat signals. Since the heartbeat sender only starts after the setup is done, and the Copy Listing task sometimes takes longer than 10 minutes, it causes timeouts. To fix this, the Copy Listing task was moved to the output committer’s setup, which happens after the heartbeat sender has already started, preventing timeouts.

---

# Conclusion

Looking ahead, the team is focusing on several enhancements centered on parallelization, better resource utilization, and network management. These include:

- Parallelizing file permission setting
- Parallelizing input splitting
- Moving compute-intensive commit tasks to the Reduce phase to boost scalability
- Implementing a dynamic bandwidth throttler

Additionally, we plan to contribute an open-source patch for these optimizations. The Uber Hivesync team remains focused on solving data replication challenges, where even small improvements can lead to significant gains at our scale.

podcast_script:
<Person1>Welcome back to the show. Today we’re going to unpack a really practical scaling story from Uber Engineering: how they scaled data replication to move petabytes every day. This is about their data lake—350-plus petabytes—spread across on-prem data centers and cloud, with strict disaster recovery and freshness requirements. And the core of the story is: they were using Hadoop DistCp, it worked… until it didn’t, and then they had to re-architect the hot spots without rewriting everything from scratch.</Person1>

<Person2>Petabytes every day is already a wild baseline. But what I like about this topic is it’s not just “add more machines.” It’s about where the bottlenecks show up when you have hundreds of thousands of replication jobs and you’re operating inside real constraints—network bandwidth, NameNode load, YARN queues, JVM overhead. So, what’s the system we’re talking about? Is this like a generic file copy service?</Person2>

<Person1>It’s more specific. Uber has a service called HiveSync. It keeps Hive datasets replicated between regions—both between HDFS clusters and between HDFS and cloud object storage—so that data is available in more than one geography. That matters for disaster recovery, for multi-region access patterns, and for a big strategic move: migrating their data lake from on-prem to cloud regions.</Person1>

<Person2>And HiveSync uses DistCp underneath?</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. DistCp—Distributed Copy—is a Hadoop tool that copies large datasets in parallel using MapReduce. The idea is simple: list the files to copy, split them into chunks, distribute those chunks across mappers, copy in parallel, and then commit the results at the destination.</Person1>

<Person2>Okay, so the “distributed” part is MapReduce mappers doing the copying. But the listing and planning—how distributed is that?</Person2>

<Person1>That question turns out to be the heart of the scaling problem. In the open-source DistCp flow, a lot of the preparation work is done client-side before the job even really gets going. DistCp on the client identifies files, builds what’s called the Copy Listing—a sequence file describing the chunks to copy—then the Hadoop client does input splitting to assign chunks to mappers, and only then does it submit a job to YARN.</Person1>

<Person2>So if you have a lot of jobs, you’re doing a lot of file system metadata work on the client, potentially in a multi-threaded service that’s submitting jobs constantly.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. Uber’s HiveSync server is that multi-threaded service. It receives replication requests from a source Hive cluster, and for larger transfers—over about 256 megabytes—it submits DistCp jobs. It has multiple worker threads preparing and submitting these jobs in parallel, plus monitoring threads tracking progress.</Person1>

<Person2>And then scale changed.</Person2>

<Person1>Scale changed dramatically. By Q3 2022, their daily replicated volume surged from about 250 terabytes per day to 1 petabyte per day—in a single quarter. At the same time, the number of datasets under HiveSync jumped from 30,000 to 144,000. And the number of daily replication jobs went from 10,000 to an average of 374,000.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s not a linear increase. That’s a different universe.</Person2>

<Person1>Right, and it pushed them past their ability to keep up with SLAs. They had a P100 replication lag SLA of 4 hours and a P99.9 SLO of 20 minutes. With a backlog of jobs, those numbers become impossible.</Person1>

<Person2>What triggered this spike? Just organic growth?</Person2>

<Person1>Partly, but there were architectural shifts. Uber moved toward an active-passive data lake architecture for cost savings. Instead of evenly distributed data generation, one primary on-prem region handled roughly 90% of data generation and most batch compute. That concentrated writes and replication demand: everything needed to be copied from primary to secondary for disaster recovery.</Person1>

<Person2>So HiveSync becomes the DR pipeline. If it falls behind, your recovery point objective gets worse.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. And then they onboarded essentially their entire on-prem Hive footprint into HiveSync, making it a universal mechanism for cross-region replication. Plus, they anticipated even more load because HiveSync would be central for on-prem-to-cloud migration, which would nearly double volume and request scale again.</Person1>

<Person2>So they needed DistCp to scale not just in throughput of copying, but in job orchestration overhead.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. And the blog is really about pinpointing where DistCp stops scaling in this environment and then making targeted changes. Uber’s improvements fall into three big buckets:

1) Move the resource-intensive preparation tasks—Copy Listing and Input Splitting—from the HiveSync server to the YARN Application Master.
2) Parallelize Copy Listing and parallelize Copy Committer.
3) Use “Uber jobs” for small transfers to avoid container launches and JVM startup overhead.

And then, they also improved observability and handled rollout challenges like OOMs, YARN queue saturation, and AM heartbeat timeouts.</Person1>

<Person2>Let’s go step by step. First, the contention problem on the HiveSync server. What was happening?</Person2>

<Person1>Under heavy load, they saw file system latency increases that directly increased DistCp Copy Listing latency. They looked at thread dumps and found many threads blocked waiting on a lock held by the HDFS client for RPC calls.</Person1>

<Person2>So it’s JVM-level lock contention inside the HDFS client library?</Person2>

<Person1>That’s the idea. In a typical DistCp submission flow, multiple components are using the same HDFS client: HiveSync worker threads doing comparisons, the DistCp tool doing Copy Listing, and the Hadoop client doing Input Splitting. As they increased executor threads to handle more replication requests, they increased the number of concurrent users of that shared HDFS client. The HDFS client’s internal synchronization becomes a bottleneck.</Person1>

<Person2>And Copy Listing is metadata-heavy: listing files, calling the NameNode, getting block locations. That’s a lot of RPC.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. The number of NameNode calls is proportional to the number of files being copied, which is particularly painful for large directories. Uber found that Copy Listing alone was responsible for about 90% of job submission latency.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s huge, because it means the cluster might have capacity to copy, but you can’t even get jobs into YARN fast enough because the client is stuck planning them.</Person2>

<Person1>Right. So their first major change: shift Copy Listing and Input Splitting from the HiveSync server to the Application Master. In other words, instead of the client doing all that pre-work and then submitting a job, they submit a job that does the listing inside the AM container itself.</Person1>

<Person2>So each job now has its own isolated HDFS client context in the AM, rather than hundreds of threads contending on one JVM’s client locks.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. That reduces lock contention on the HiveSync server’s Hadoop client. Uber reports this reduced DistCp job submission latency by around 90%.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s a big win. But I’m thinking: doesn’t that increase work inside the AM and potentially make the AM heavier and more failure-prone?</Person2>

<Person1>It does shift load. And they did encounter issues—like out-of-memory exceptions in the AM and also timeouts related to AM heartbeats. But the core trade-off is worth it because it unblocks job submission and isolates per-job planning overhead.</Person1>

<Person2>Let’s talk about the next bottleneck: even after moving listing into the AM, listing can still be slow because it’s sequential. What did they do there?</Person2>

<Person1>They parallelized Copy Listing. In open-source DistCp, Copy Listing generates filesystem blocks for files to be copied and writes them to a sequence file. The main thread calls the NameNode using getFileBlockLocations for each file larger than a chunk size, creates file splits, retries on failures—so it’s both RPC-heavy and sequential.</Person1>

<Person2>So if you have directories with tons of files, you’re doing these calls one by one, and tail latency gets ugly.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. Uber observed p99 listing latency around 10 minutes on the busiest replication server even after moving the task to the AM. They noticed an important property: multiple files can be listed in parallel and written in any order to the sequence file, but the chunks of each individual file need to stay together and in order. That’s because the Copy Committer later uses that ordering to merge chunks into final files.</Person1>

<Person2>So they can parallelize per-file split generation, as long as they preserve intra-file ordering when writing.</Person2>

<Person1>Right. Their approach: assign a separate thread to create splits for each file. Those threads push results into a blocking queue. Then a separate writer thread polls the queue and writes the blocks to the sequence file in the required order for each file.

They also designed for fast failure: if any listing thread fails, the main thread stops the process and retries the DistCp job.</Person1>

<Person2>And what was the improvement?</Person2>

<Person1>Using six threads, they achieved about a 60% reduction in p99 average listing latency and about a 75% decrease in maximum latency across HiveSync servers.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s a classic “parallelize the metadata plane” optimization. We talk a lot about data plane throughput—copy bandwidth—but metadata plane can dominate at scale.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. And then there’s the commit side. After the mappers copy chunks, DistCp has to merge them at the destination. That’s the Copy Committer task.</Person1>

<Person2>Which in open source is sequential concatenation?</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. The Copy Committer runs in the AM and merges copied splits into full files. For directories with more than 500,000 files, this could take up to 30 minutes. Again, that’s unacceptable if you’re trying to meet tight replication lag SLOs and you’re running hundreds of thousands of jobs.</Person1>

<Person2>So they parallelized the concatenation too?</Person2>

<Person1>They did. They used multiple threads, each responsible for merging one file at a time. The sequence file produced during Copy Listing identifies the order of blocks for each file; concatenator threads gather the splits for a file and merge them into the final output file.

They again built in fast failure: if any thread hits an issue, the main thread halts and retries the job.</Person1>

<Person2>And the numbers?</Person2>

<Person1>They report mean concat latency dropped by 97.29% using 10 threads.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s massive. So now we have: faster submission, faster listing, faster commit. But there’s another issue: job overhead for tiny copies.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. Uber found that around 52% of DistCp jobs submitted by HiveSync only needed one mapper—copying less than 512 megabytes and fewer than 200 files. These jobs finish quickly, but they spend a disproportionate amount of time on YARN container allocation, environment setup, and JVM startup rather than actual copying.</Person1>

<Person2>So the fixed overhead dominates. And with hundreds of thousands of jobs, that overhead becomes your capacity ceiling.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. Their solution was to “uberize” single-mapper jobs using Hadoop’s Uber jobs feature. An Uber job runs tasks directly within the Application Master JVM instead of launching separate containers for map tasks.</Person1>

<Person2>So you avoid container launch overhead entirely for those small jobs.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. The AM decides whether the job qualifies. If it does, it executes the Copy Mapper locally in the AM’s JVM. If not, it requests containers and runs mappers normally.

They enabled this with configs like:
- mapreduce.job.ubertask.enable=true
- mapreduce.job.ubertask.maxmaps=1
- mapreduce.job.ubertask.maxbytes=512MB

The impact: they eliminated around 268,000 single-core container launches per day.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s not just faster jobs. That’s freeing up YARN scheduling capacity, reducing queue pressure, lowering NodeManager churn—lots of second-order benefits.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. Now, what did all of this add up to? The blog calls out three major impact areas.

First: a 5x boost in incremental replication capacity across on-prem data centers over one year, without scaling incidents.

Second: enabling bulk migration from on-prem to cloud. They’ve migrated over 306 petabytes to the cloud using HiveSync, and these DistCp enhancements were crucial to handle that scale.

Third: better observability. They introduced metrics around job submission times on both client and AM sides, submission rates, Copy Listing and Copy Committer performance, max heap usage of containers, p99 copy rate per job, and overall copy rate. That visibility helped them mitigate and diagnose incidents.</Person1>

<Person2>Observability is often the unsung hero. When you change where work runs—from client to AM, from sequential to parallel—you introduce new failure modes. Without metrics, you’re blind.</Person2>

<Person1>Totally. And they did hit challenges during rollout.

One was AM OOMs. Making the AM do more work and sometimes run the mapper locally increases memory pressure. They addressed it with stress testing to identify optimal memory and core usage, and added metrics specifically to detect OOM issues. That later helped them tune YARN resource configurations for memory-intensive copy requests.</Person1>

<Person2>Another challenge I’d expect: if you reduce submission latency by 90%, you can accidentally create a thundering herd against YARN.</Person2>

<Person1>They saw exactly that. Lower submission latency increased the job submission rate, leading to “Yarn Queue Full” errors. Their mitigation was a circuit breaker in HiveSync: it temporarily pauses new submissions until retries succeed. And they added metrics to detect circuit breaker events, enabling real-time monitoring and adjustments to YARN queue capacity.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s a nice example of systems thinking: improving one bottleneck can expose the next one, and you need a control mechanism—backpressure—to keep the system stable.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. They also mention network bandwidth as a constraint. Higher copy rates can drive high bandwidth usage, so they had to tune to balance performance with limits.</Person1>

<Person2>And you mentioned AM heartbeat timeouts earlier. What happened there?</Person2>

<Person1>When they first moved Copy Listing and Input Splitting into the AM, they put it in the setup phase of the AM. But YARN’s ResourceManager expects the AM to send regular heartbeat signals. The heartbeat sender starts only after setup is done. If Copy Listing runs longer than about 10 minutes, the AM doesn’t heartbeat in time, so the RM times it out and kills it.</Person1>

<Person2>So the AM is doing useful work but appears dead to YARN.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. Their fix: move Copy Listing to the output committer’s setup, which occurs after the heartbeat sender has started. That way, long-running listing doesn’t prevent heartbeats and avoids timeouts.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s a very Hadoop-specific gotcha, and it’s great they called it out. It shows how moving code around in the lifecycle isn’t just a refactor—it changes the contract with the scheduler.</Person2>

<Person1>Absolutely. Now, I want to zoom out and talk about the architectural pattern here, because it’s broadly applicable.

Uber didn’t replace DistCp. They kept the core data plane—mappers copying blocks—but optimized the control plane: planning, scheduling overhead, and commit. They reduced contention on shared clients, parallelized metadata-heavy steps, and avoided expensive scheduling paths for small jobs.</Person1>

<Person2>It’s like they identified three main sources of wasted time:
1) Client-side lock contention and NameNode RPC serialization.
2) Sequential algorithms in listing and commit phases.
3) Fixed per-job overhead in YARN for small work units.

And then they attacked each one with a targeted change.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. And the numbers show why that matters. When you’re running 374,000 jobs a day, even a small constant overhead per job becomes enormous. Also, tail latency matters because SLAs like P99.9 and P100 are dominated by the slowest jobs—often the ones with huge directories or pathological metadata patterns.</Person1>

<Person2>Let’s connect this back to DistCp’s original architecture for listeners who haven’t used it. Can you walk through the components quickly?</Person2>

<Person1>Sure. High-level:
- The DistCp tool identifies files, groups them into blocks in Copy Listing, defines distribution across mappers, and submits a job.
- The Hadoop client sets up the job environment and does input splitting.
- YARN’s ResourceManager schedules tasks and allocates containers.
- The Application Master monitors the job, requests containers for copy mappers, and consolidates at destination.
- Copy Mapper tasks copy assigned blocks.
- Copy Committer merges blocks into final files.

Uber’s main changes were to relocate and parallelize some of these steps and to run small mappers inside the AM.</Person1>

<Person2>And HiveSync itself: it’s a service layer that takes replication requests, decides whether to do bulk or incremental replication, and for large transfers submits DistCp jobs through an executor with multiple worker threads.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. HiveSync was initially built on Airbnb’s ReAir project and supports both bulk and incremental replication. So it’s not just a one-off copy tool; it’s a continuous replication system for Hive datasets.</Person1>

<Person2>Now I’m curious: when they parallelized Copy Listing, they said chunks of each file must be kept together and in order because Copy Committer relies on it. That implies the committer algorithm expects a certain sequence layout. Did they change the committer algorithm too?</Person2>

<Person1>They didn’t fundamentally change the algorithm’s dependency on ordering; they changed how they generate the sequence file while preserving per-file ordering. The writer thread ensures that chunks for a given file are written in the correct order. Then later, the committer can still read the sequence file and know which splits belong to which file and in what order.

And then separately, they parallelized the committer’s concatenation step while still respecting that order within each file.</Person1>

<Person2>So they preserved correctness constraints while introducing concurrency around independent units—files.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. That’s often the key to safe parallelization: find the unit of independence.

Now, looking ahead, Uber lists future work too. They want to parallelize file permission setting, parallelize input splitting, move compute-intensive commit tasks to the Reduce phase for better scalability, and implement dynamic bandwidth throttling. And they plan to contribute an open-source patch for these optimizations.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s interesting—moving commit tasks to the Reduce phase. That suggests using reducers as a scalable parallel stage rather than doing everything in the AM, which can become a single-node bottleneck.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. Right now, even with multithreading, the AM is still one container. If you need to scale commit beyond that, you might distribute it via reducers. But that’s a more invasive change.

Dynamic bandwidth throttling is also important because when you successfully speed everything up, you can saturate inter-DC links. Throttling becomes a first-class control loop.</Person1>

<Person2>So, big takeaway: scaling replication isn’t just about throughput; it’s about the entire lifecycle of a replication job—planning, scheduling, execution, commit, and the feedback loops that keep the system stable.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. And the story is a great reminder that open-source tools are often built for generality and modest scale, and at Uber scale you find the edges: lock contention in clients, sequential metadata operations, and scheduler overhead.

If you’re building something similar, a mental checklist would be:
- Where is metadata work happening, and is it centralized?
- Are there sequential phases that dominate tail latency?
- What percentage of jobs are “small,” and what is the fixed cost per job?
- What happens to the rest of the system when you remove a bottleneck—do you need backpressure?
- Do you have the metrics to see each phase separately?</Person1>

<Person2>And don’t forget lifecycle quirks like AM heartbeats—when you move code into the AM, you inherit YARN’s expectations about when the AM becomes “alive.”</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. That’s the episode. We covered how Uber:
- Moved Copy Listing and Input Splitting into the AM to reduce client contention and cut submission latency by about 90%.
- Parallelized Copy Listing to cut p99 listing latency by about 60% and reduce worst-case latency by about 75%.
- Parallelized Copy Committer concatenation to reduce mean concat latency by about 97%.
- Used Uber jobs for single-mapper copies under 512MB to eliminate roughly 268,000 container launches per day.

And the broader impact: 5x incremental replication capacity and 306PB migrated to the cloud.

<Person2>Next time you hear “we just run DistCp,” remember: at petabyte-per-day scale, the real work is making DistCp behave like a high-throughput service component, not a command-line batch tool.</Person2>

<Person1>Well said. Thanks for listening, and we’ll see you in the next one.</Person1>

title: Apache Hudi™ at Uber: Engineering for Trillion-Record-Scale Data Lake Operations | Uber Blog
summary: Uber built Apache Hudi to bring database-like primitives—ACID transactions, indexing for fast upserts, and incremental processing—into a file-based data lake so that highly mutable, high-volume datasets could stay query-ready with minute-level freshness. Operating the world’s largest Hudi deployment (19,500 datasets, ~10 PB ingested/day, ~350K commits/day, and tables >400B rows), Uber had to engineer beyond typical lakehouse limits, introducing the Metadata Table to eliminate filesystem listing bottlenecks, a native Record Index for O(1) record-key lookups at trillion-row scale, and multi-region sync for resilience and cloud migration. The key insight is that trillion-record lake operations are won as much by indexing, metadata acceleration, observability, and config governance as by storage format—turning “files in object storage/HDFS” into an operationally reliable, continuously mutable platform.

# The Foundation of Uber’s Data Platform

Uber operates one of the most diverse and demanding data ecosystems in the world. Every trip taken, order delivered, ad served, or real-time arrival time recalculated generates an unending stream of data. These data points come from hundreds of microservices, thousands of cities, and millions of riders, each with its own velocity, shape, and business-criticality. At the heart of this ecosystem lies Uber’s data lake: a multi-hundred-petabyte repository that fuels operational decisions, machine learning models, experimentation platforms, and real-time business intelligence.

Powering this data lake requires far more than storing large volumes of data. Constant mutation, high cardinality, fast-changing schemas, and a relentless requirement for data freshness characterize Uber’s workloads. Many of Uber’s teams require data that isn’t only large in scale (trillions of rows in a single dataset), but also accurate within minutes, not hours.

This combination, unprecedented scale, relentless freshness, and high operational rigor, created a unique challenge that existing data lake technologies couldn’t meet at the time. And this challenge led to the birth of Apache Hudi™, a data lake storage engine designed and built at Uber. Hudi introduced the industry to a new paradigm: bringing database-like primitives (ACID transactions, indexing, incremental processing) directly to the data lake while retaining its scalability and flexibility. Since then, Hudi has grown into a critical pillar of Uber’s data platform, powering tens of thousands of datasets, handling massive daily ingestion volumes, and providing the consistency and freshness guarantees required by virtually every business line at Uber, from mobility and delivery to ads, marketplace, safety, and maps.

In this blog, we’ll take readers behind the scenes of the world’s largest Hudi deployment and one of the largest centralized data lakes on the planet, how Hudi was born at Uber, and how it evolved to handle trillion-record workloads. We’ll also show how Hudi fits into Uber’s broader data platform, the scale at which it operates, and the lessons we’ve learned after nearly a decade of managing one of the most demanding data lakes ever built.

## **Why We Built Hudi**

By 2015, Uber’s data systems were [expanding faster](https://www.uber.com/blog/uber-big-data-platform/) than any off-the-shelf technology could keep up with. We were ingesting data from thousands of microservices, dozens of new product lines, and a rapidly globalizing footprint. Data wasn’t only increasing in volume—it was constantly changing, with new events, backfills, late-arriving updates, and fast-evolving schemas becoming the norm. At that time, the industry standard data lake was fundamentally file-based, append-only, and batch-oriented. These architectures worked well for static or slowly evolving datasets, but they fell short on the workloads that mattered most to Uber:

- Mutable data models (trip life cycle, order states, device attributes, account changes)
- High-volume streams with high update rates
- End-to-end incremental processing to avoid full-table recomputation

No available solution provided a reliable way to maintain fresh, query-ready tables while supporting ACID guarantees, fast upserts, and near-real-time ingestion of frequently changing data. We needed the performance characteristics of a database system without giving up the openness, flexibility, and scale of the data lake. This gap is what led Uber engineers to build Hudi, a new class of storage engine purpose-built for the data lake. Instead of treating the lake as passive file storage, Hudi added the three primitives that fundamentally changed how data lakes operate:

- ACID transactions. Ensuring correctness despite concurrency, retries, late data, or overlapping pipelines.
- Indexing and fast upserts. Making it possible to update rows efficiently across hundreds of partitions and billions of records.
- Incremental processing. Allowing pipelines to read only what changed since the last commit instead of scanning terabytes each time.

Even as open-source Hudi has matured, Uber’s internal scale has pushed far beyond its original boundaries. That pressure led to innovations such as the Metadata Table, Record Index, multi-data-center sync, and highly optimized table services.

### Apache Hudi Community and Ecosystem Highlights

The Apache Hudi community has had a broad reach, with 6,030 GitHub stars, over 15,000 members across Hudi Slack® and GitHub®, and 17,500 followers across LinkedIn® and X®.

The project has also seen large-scale activity, with over 10,000 pull requests, 6,500 issues, 7,500 Slack messages resolved, and over 85,000 GitHub interactions (comments, reviews, and so on).

According to ossinsight.io, there are over 50 contributing companies to the ecosystem. Over 36,000 companies visit the Hudi website, including 65% of Fortune 500 companies. Further, 10 cloud providers support Hudi, including the big three of AWS®*,* Microsoft Azure®, and GCP (Google Cloud Platform™ *)*.

For project governance, there are 20 PMC members. Contributions are large, with 41 committers and over 2,000 contributors.

---

## **Data at Uber Scale**

To understand why Hudi is indispensable at Uber, it’s important to appreciate the scale at which our data platform operates. Uber doesn’t just run a large data lake; we run one of the largest and most diverse production data lake environments ever built, with data patterns spanning mobility, delivery, freight, mapping, ads, pricing, safety, experimentation, and real-time machine learning. Every one of these workloads has its own shape, its own cadence, and its own tolerance for latency. Hudi sits at the center of this ecosystem, orchestrating billions of records and thousands of tables with strong guarantees of correctness and freshness.

### Hudi’s Daily Footprint at Uber

Here are the high-level aggregate metrics that represent the magnitude of operations Hudi supports within Uber’s data platform.

- 19,500 Hudi datasets managed across multiple business domains
- 6 trillion rows across ingested per day and 3 million new data files added
- 350 logical petabytes on HDFS™ and Google Cloud Storage
- 10 petabytes ingested per day and over 3 petabytes written to the data lake
- 350,000 commits per day
- 70,000 table service operations daily (compactions, cleans, clustering)
- 4 million analytical queries served via Presto*®* and Apache Spark™ per week
- Ultra-large tables exceeding 400 billions rows that update continuously
- The majority of datasets are partitioned by date, optimized for large-scale distributed processing

These numbers highlight a simple truth: no other open data lake technology had been tested at this scale before. Running Hudi at Uber effectively meant inventing new operational patterns, new indexing systems, and new table services—many of which have since influenced the open-source roadmap.

Every dataset at Uber maps to one of several workload classes, each with unique operational behaviors, which drive how Hudi is configured and tuned.

### Append-Only Datasets (11,200 tables)

Append-only tables constitute the majority of Uber’s datasets and are responsible for the highest data ingestion volume. These tables, which include data from raw ingestion sources, use Apache Hudi’s bulk insert capabilities to efficiently ingest billions of rows and thousands of Parquet files into the data lake. The speed of these bulk inserts is a critical factor, directly determining the freshness SLA offered to data lake users while simultaneously ensuring optimal file sizing and a query-friendly data layout.

### Upsert-Heavy Datasets (4,400 tables)

Upsert datasets model changing business states, sometimes with very high update frequencies or sparse updates in enormous datasets. These tables use index-based lookups to achieve high write throughput and fast commits.

### Derived Datasets (1,600 tables)

Derived datasets power Uber’s analytics and machine learning ecosystem through transformations, joins, and aggregations of raw Hudi datasets. We use both Spark batch jobs and Hudi Streamer to run these pipelines, using [incremental reads](https://www.uber.com/en-US/blog/ubers-lakehouse-architecture/) to process only new or changed data from upstream tables. Freshness remains important, but correctness and schema evolution become equally critical, especially when the upstream tables may themselves get rolled back or fixed. Even though the source tables can be append-only, the derived tables doing incremental aggregations move to a mutable data model. So, having data lake storage that’s upsert-optimized is crucial at Uber.

### Realtime Ingestion (Flink®-Native Streaming) (500 tables)

Uber has been steadily shifting a portion of ingestion from Spark-based batch jobs to Flink®-native streamingto support sub-15-minute freshness SLOs. Real-time ingestion is designed for very high-throughput, low-latency writes as the primary goal, and various data optimizations (file stitching, data sorting, data encryption) are applied asynchronously through our Table Services Platform continuously without blocking ingestion.

This workload diversity is one of the defining characteristics of Uber’s data platform and a key reason Hudi evolved beyond standard open-source capabilities.

*Running Hudi at Uber is a continuous journey of scaling, optimizing, and engineering beyond established boundaries.*

---

## **Uber’s Data Lake Technology Stack**

Hudi is a foundational component of Uber’s data lake, but its real power lies in its seamless integration with the rest of Uber’s data platform. At Uber’s scale, no system can exist as a standalone component. Instead, Hudi participates in a deeply interconnected stack spanning ingestion, orchestration, compute, querying, observability, and downstream ML and analytics platforms.

This section provides a high-level view of that architecture and explains how Hudi fits into it.

### Ingestion Layer

Spark Batch Ingestion is used for large-scale, hourly, or daily pipelines, handling backfills, reprocessing, and high-throughput batch writes. It powers bulk inserts into both append-only and upsert tables and has been extensively optimized using custom serializers and shuffle strategies. The ingestion process writes directly into Hudi tables, relying on Hudi’s commit protocol to produce atomic, consistent snapshots. The thousands of pipelines writing to Hudi tables are managed by an internal scheduler called Piper, which executes DAGs, manages retries, enforces SLAs, and tracks failures.

Flink Streaming Ingestion is designed for continuous, low-latency writes and supports event-time ordering, watermarking, and high-concurrency pipelines. It’s ideal for workloads requiring sub-15-minute freshness and is enabling Uber’s shift to streaming-native ingestion across critical business lines. Streaming ingestion is a significant focus area for Uber for achieving data freshness targets in minutes.

### Query Layer: Spark, Presto, and Downstream Platforms

Two analytical engines dominate query access to Hudi tables at Uber: Apache Spark and Presto. Apache Spark is heavily used for complex data engineering and ML workflows, including ETL pipelines, feature engineering, and distributed batch ML workloads. A deeply optimized integration between Spark and Hudi enables fast scans, pruning, and crucial incremental data processing via Hudi’s commit timeline.

In contrast, Presto is the go-to engine for high-concurrency, interactive analytics.

### Observability and Monitoring

Managing over 18,000 datasets requires first-class observability, which Uber achieves by pairing Hudi with a comprehensive monitoring ecosystem. All Hudi operations emit M3-based metrics tracking events like read/write performance, commit latency, compaction throughput, file/growth rates, and even table health. An alerting system queries the metrics to address SLA misses, storage regressions, out-of-bound growth, and indexing or Metadata Table failures. This robust observability layer is essential for the operational excellence needed to manage a trillion-scale data lake.

---

Figure 1: Uber’s data lake architecture with Hudi at the core.
