author: Dhyanam Vaidya
podcast_script:
<Person1>Today we’re unpacking a problem that hits every large-scale distributed system eventually: database overload. And not the simple kind where you just add replicas or increase a connection pool, but overload that cascades—where a small spike becomes timeouts, retries, and then a full-on incident.</Person1>

<Person2>And we’re using Uber as the case study, right? They run thousands of microservices and have these in-house databases—Docstore and Schemaless—built on top of MySQL.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. The blog we’re covering is Uber’s journey from static rate limiting to what they call “intelligent load management.” The key idea is that at Uber’s scale—tens of millions of requests per second, thousands of clusters, tens of petabytes—overload isn’t isolated. A brief hotspot can ripple outward across services and tenants.</Person1>

<Person2>So we’re not just talking about protecting a single database instance. We’re talking about protecting a fleet, and also protecting fairness. Because in a multitenant platform, one noisy tenant can consume shared resources and degrade everyone else.</Person2>

<Person1>Right. And Uber’s databases are both mission-critical and latency-sensitive. They back rides, deliveries, maps, payments—basically everything. So overload protection has to do two things at once: keep the system stable under global stress, and also prevent “noisy neighbor” scenarios even when the system isn’t globally overloaded.</Person1>

<Person2>Let’s set the stage with architecture first. What are Docstore and Schemaless, structurally?</Person2>

<Person1>They share a common foundation. There’s a stateless query engine layer and a stateful storage engine layer, plus a control plane. For this discussion, the important parts are query and storage.

The stateless query engine does routing, query planning, sharding, schema management, auth, parsing, validation—basically it’s the front door and traffic coordinator.</Person1>

<Person2>And the storage layer?</Person2>

<Person1>The storage engine is stateful: transaction management, connection pooling, replication, consensus. Data is sharded into partitions. Each partition has one leader and two followers coordinated via Raft for strong consistency. Underneath, each partition is backed by MySQL nodes with NVMe SSDs.</Person1>

<Person2>So already: strong consistency, Raft replication, MySQL storage, and then a big stateless routing tier in front. That’s a common pattern. But it sets up a question: where do you enforce overload protection? At the edge, or close to the state?</Person2>

<Person1>And that question is at the heart of the story. Uber initially tried to enforce overload protection in the query engine layer using quota-based rate limiting.</Person1>

<Person2>That sounds straightforward: give each tenant a quota, measure request cost, and reject when they exceed it.</Person2>

<Person1>That was the idea. They assigned “capacity unit” costs to reads and writes based on bytes processed, gave each user a quota, and returned HTTP 429 when exceeded. Because query routers are stateless, they stored quota usage in a central Redis cache.</Person1>

<Person2>And it didn’t work out.</Person2>

<Person1>It didn’t hold up in production. The blog outlines several reasons.

First: complexity and overhead. Every request required a Redis call—extra network hop, extra dependency, extra failure mode.</Person1>

<Person2>At Uber scale, “one extra hop” is not a detail. It’s a system-wide tax.</Person2>

<Person1>Second: the query layer would need real-time health and load info for thousands of partitions to shed traffic accurately when a specific storage partition is overloaded. That’s a huge tracking problem, and it undermines the scalability of keeping the routing layer stateless.</Person1>

<Person2>So the edge layer doesn’t have the source of truth. It’s guessing, and the guessing gets expensive.</Person2>

<Person1>Third: the cost model was too imprecise. MySQL behavior makes it hard to infer true cost from bytes returned. A full table scan that returns one row might look “small” in terms of returned bytes, but it’s massively expensive. They ended up metering lightweight and heavyweight operations similarly, making enforcement unreliable.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s a classic pitfall: measuring the wrong thing. You can’t infer CPU, I/O, or lock contention from a simple output size metric.</Person2>

<Person1>And fourth: quotas were static. Stakeholders constantly asked for adjustments. In a multitenant, dynamic environment, static quotas become operational toil and don’t reflect reality.</Person1>

<Person2>So they learned: overload management needs to live close to the storage nodes, where the real signals are.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. That became a cornerstone principle: place control close to the source of truth.

Now, before we get into what they built, there’s another key concept they highlight: choosing the right signal for overload.</Person1>

<Person2>Because the naive approach is QPS-based rate limiting.</Person2>

<Person1>Right. But QPS is too coarse. Different queries have different costs. And even the same query can have different latencies depending on cache, contention, or data distribution.

Uber points out that concurrency—the number of in-flight operations—is often a better signal. It’s tied to Little’s Law: Concurrency = Throughput × Latency. If latency rises, concurrency rises for the same throughput. Concurrency maps more directly to resource usage in a stateful system.</Person1>

<Person2>So concurrency is like a live indicator that the system is spending more time per request, and therefore accumulating more work in flight.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. And they also emphasize fairness. You need two parallel capabilities:

1) Resilience under global stress: shed by priority, dropping low-priority first.
2) Noisy-neighbor control: per-tenant limits even when the system is otherwise healthy.

That dual requirement shapes the architecture they built.</Person1>

<Person2>Okay, so what was their first serious storage-layer approach?</Person2>

<Person1>They built a unified load manager foundation using a combination of:

- CoDel queues for load shedding
- A Scorecard engine for per-tenant concurrency fairness
- Plug-in regulators for specific overload invariants

Let’s break those down.</Person1>

<Person2>Start with CoDel. That’s from networking—Controlled Delay—used to fight bufferbloat.</Person2>

<Person1>Right. Traditional queue management often looks at queue length. CoDel looks at queueing delay—how long requests are waiting. It tries to keep latency bounded by shedding when waiting time grows.

Uber implemented separate CoDel queues by operation type:

- Read queue: point lookups and light queries
- Write queue: insert/update/upsert
- Slow queue: long-running/background ops like scans, deletes, replication

Each queue managed independently, giving isolation between workloads.</Person1>

<Person2>And CoDel has that interesting behavior: FIFO under normal load, but under pressure it switches to LIFO, right?</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. FIFO alone can be a trap during overload. Old requests pile up, they wait too long, clients abandon them or time out and retry, creating wasted work. And meanwhile, new requests that might succeed quickly get stuck behind stale ones.

CoDel’s adaptive LIFO prioritizes newer requests under overload. That helps the system “fail fast” on stale work and keep responsiveness for fresh requests.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s counterintuitive at first—LIFO feels unfair—but in overload it can be the difference between recovering and spiraling.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. It’s a practical fairness: fairness to success probability and user experience, not fairness to arrival order.

Now, CoDel was load shedding. But it didn’t solve multitenant fairness by itself. That’s where Scorecard comes in.</Person1>

<Person2>Scorecard is like admission control per tenant?</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. It’s a rule-based admission control component and a lightweight quota system that enforces per-tenant concurrency limits. The key: it’s deterministic and simple. It’s designed for incident containment—when a tenant misbehaves, you cap them quickly without hurting everyone else.</Person1>

<Person2>So even if the system isn’t globally overloaded, a tenant can’t monopolize concurrency slots.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. And it helps on-call by making the blast radius smaller and making it easier to identify the source of disruption.

But concurrency isn’t the only way to overload a stateful system. Some overload patterns don’t show up as concurrency saturation.</Person1>

<Person2>Like huge payload writes at low QPS, or hotspotting a partition key.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. So they added regulators—plug-in node-local overload detectors that enforce invariants. These are meant to trigger rarely in healthy operation, but to save the system during skewed behavior.

Their regulators included:

- Write bytes regulator: limit concurrent write volume to avoid I/O saturation
- Partition key regulator: throttle hot partition keys
- Memory regulator: throttle when free process memory is low
- Goroutines regulator: throttle when goroutine count exceeds threshold

This is very pragmatic. In Go services, goroutine explosion during overload can kill you.</Person1>

<Person2>So at this stage, they have: CoDel queues to shed, Scorecard to enforce per-tenant concurrency, and regulators to catch edge-case overload modes.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. And it worked in important ways. They improved stability and availability by shedding excess requests and preventing runaway resource exhaustion. And Scorecard isolated misbehaving tenants.

But there were limitations.</Person1>

<Person2>Let me guess: CoDel treated all requests equally, so it could drop high-priority user-facing traffic along with low-priority background jobs.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. Priority-agnostic shedding leads to bad customer experience and increased on-call load.

Also, CoDel relied on fixed queue timeouts and static inflight concurrency limits. In a dynamic system, static tuning is low-fidelity and creates operational toil.</Person1>

<Person2>And fixed wait times can cause thundering herd effects: requests wait, then get rejected around the same time, then retry together, causing repeated overload cycles.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. During those cycles, without traffic differentiation, even high-priority requests get dropped, amplifying the blast radius.

So the system “kept things from breaking” but lacked nuance and dynamism.</Person1>

<Person2>That sets up the next evolution: Cinnamon.</Person2>

<Person1>Right. They replaced CoDel with Cinnamon, a priority-aware load shedder originally developed by Uber’s Delivery team.

The key observation was that many overloads came from low-priority async jobs—pipelines, aggregators, internal garbage collection. Those shouldn’t have the same survivability as ride requests or real-time pricing queries.</Person1>

<Person2>So Cinnamon ranks requests and sheds low priority first.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. It uses a tiering model from tier 0 to tier 5.

- t0 is reserved for a small subset of critical infrastructure
- t1 is the most important user-facing online traffic—the core workloads to protect
- t5 is least critical

Request rank comes from request-attached priority, or a default based on the calling service if the request doesn’t specify priority.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s crucial: you need a workable defaulting mechanism, otherwise priority systems collapse because nobody sets priority correctly.</Person2>

<Person1>Right. With priority awareness, they simplified the queue structure: just read and write queues. Long-running/background operations were marked lower priority instead of being routed to a separate “slow queue.”</Person1>

<Person2>So the differentiation becomes semantic—priority—rather than hard-coded queue topology.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly.

Now, Cinnamon also addressed the static tuning issue. It adapts queue timeout thresholds using P90 latency metrics, and it has an Auto Tuner that dynamically adjusts inflight limits—those “available slots”—to maximize throughput while staying stable.</Person1>

<Person2>And it’s PID-controlled, right? That’s a big theme in the blog: PID-based control avoids overreaction.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. CoDel’s behavior with static timeouts could be aggressive: reject after a fixed wait like 5ms. Cinnamon uses PID-based control to adjust queue timeouts and inflight limits based on real-time latency and error signals.

Instead of acting like a hammer—abrupt, spiky—PID makes it more like a dimmer switch: smooth, stable adjustments.</Person1>

<Person2>PID controllers are common in industrial control systems. The idea is you combine proportional response to current error, integral response to accumulated error over time, and derivative response to the rate of change. That helps avoid oscillations.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. And in overload management, oscillations are deadly because they create waves of retries and instability.

Uber reported that Cinnamon reduced premature shedding—meaning fewer unnecessary 429s, fewer retries, and smoother recovery.</Person1>

<Person2>So at this point, they have a much better load shedder. But the blog says there were still gaps.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. Cinnamon-based shedding was still primarily driven by local node health signals: inflight concurrency, write bytes, memory, and so on.

But in distributed systems, overload isn’t always local. A leader node might be healthy, but follower nodes could be lagging. In a Raft system, follower lag affects commit progress. They call this “commit index lag.”</Person1>

<Person2>So the leader should potentially shed traffic to let followers catch up, even if the leader itself isn’t saturated.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. Traditionally, they handled these remote shedding decisions using external token-bucket rate limiters. Those are easy to build, but at Uber scale they caused split-brain behaviors and globally suboptimal decisions.</Person1>

<Person2>Because token buckets are typically local, simplistic, and don’t integrate with priority-aware admission control. They also tend to allow bursts and then clamp down, which can exacerbate tail latency and resource spikes.</Person2>

<Person1>Right. And token-bucket-based limiting can cause memory and goroutine buildup because requests may still queue or block in the system rather than being rejected immediately in a controlled way.

So Uber’s final evolution was to turn Cinnamon from a concurrency-only shedder into a general-purpose overload control engine—a unified load shedding engine.</Person1>

<Person2>Let’s talk about what “unified” means here.</Person2>

<Person1>They centralized overload decisions into a single admission control path, enhancing Cinnamon to support pluggable external signals—like follower commit lag.

So instead of having one mechanism for local overload, another for remote overload, and separate token buckets floating around, they brought all signals into one control loop that is priority-aware.</Person1>

<Person2>And they call it BYOS—Bring Your Own Signal.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. The architecture is designed so teams can embed new overload signals and route them to the right control path.

If the pressure is systemic, the system sheds broadly by priority.
If the pressure is actor-specific—like a noisy tenant or a hot partition key—it can shed precisely by caller or key.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s an important distinction. You don’t want to solve a single-tenant hotspot by degrading all tenants. And you don’t want to solve system-wide stress by only punishing one tenant if the whole system is melting.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. The load manager becomes a “decision-making brain” that can interpret multiple signals and apply the right type of control.

Now, the payoff section in the blog is where they quantify improvements after moving from token buckets to unified Cinnamon PID-based shedding.</Person1>

<Person2>Let’s list the numbers, because they’re dramatic.</Person2>

<Person1>Under overload, they saw:

- 80% increase in throughput: average QPS 5,400 vs 3,000
- About 70% reduction in P99 latency: upsert P99 1.0s vs 3.1s
- About 93% fewer goroutines during overload: peak 10,000 vs 150,000
- About 60% lower heap usage: 1GB max vs 5–6GB spikes

That’s not just “a bit better.” That’s a fundamentally different failure mode under stress.</Person1>

<Person2>And the goroutine and heap numbers tell a story: token bucket limiters weren’t preventing the system from accumulating work in memory. Cinnamon sheds earlier, more smoothly, so the process stays lean.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. They also show commit lag behavior: token bucket produced spiky shedding patterns—again, hammer-like—while Cinnamon’s PID controller stabilized commit lag in a smoother way.</Person1>

<Person2>So the system doesn’t oscillate between “accept everything” and “reject everything.” It continuously modulates.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. And that leads into their lessons learned, which are broadly applicable.

Let’s go through them, because they’re essentially design principles for overload management in distributed systems.</Person1>

<Person2>First: prioritization is paramount.</Person2>

<Person1>Right. Decide what matters most and protect it. User-facing critical traffic should survive overload. Background jobs and batch pipelines are secondary.

Second: fail fast, don’t block. Rejecting early is better than holding requests in memory until they expire. It reduces wasted work, keeps latencies predictable, prevents OOMs, and improves resilience.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s a big cultural shift for some teams. People often think queueing is “polite.” But in overload, queueing is often just hiding the problem and making it worse.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly.

Third: PID regulation for stable shedding. Reactive shedding based only on current error rates often overcorrects too late and too hard. PID uses history and trends to stabilize control.</Person1>

<Person2>Fourth: place control close to the source of truth—typically the storage layer in stateful systems.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes.

Fifth: embrace dynamism. Avoid static configs. The system should adapt based on context.

Sixth: invest in visibility and monitoring. You need observability to tune and trust the system: what’s being shed, why, and which component is contributing to pressure.

And seventh: simplicity over complexity. This is a meta-principle: avoid overly complicated solutions like per-request Redis quota checks if they don’t scale operationally.</Person1>

<Person2>It’s interesting how “simplicity” shows up after they built something that sounds sophisticated—PID controllers, priority tiers, plug-in signals.</Person2>

<Person1>That’s a good point. The sophistication is in the control loop, but the operational model becomes simpler because you unify decision-making. Instead of a patchwork of limiters and ad hoc throttles, you have one platform with consistent behavior.</Person1>

<Person2>Let me ask a practical question: how do Scorecard and Cinnamon interact in the final world? Are they redundant?</Person2>

<Person1>They’re complementary. Cinnamon is primarily about overload shedding by priority based on system signals—local and remote—using a unified control loop.

Scorecard is about fairness and blast radius: per-tenant concurrency limits even when the overall system isn’t overloaded. That’s important because you can have a single tenant that’s “within system capacity” globally but still unfairly consuming shared resources.</Person1>

<Person2>And regulators still matter because not all overload is concurrency. You can overload I/O with big writes, or memory with certain patterns, or a single partition key with hotspot traffic.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. The blog frames regulators as invariants: conditions the system must not violate. They’re plug-ins that trigger rarely, but are critical when they do.</Person1>

<Person2>So if we zoom out: Uber started with stateless, query-layer quotas; learned that cost modeling and health tracking were too hard there; moved to storage-layer admission control; first used CoDel for queue delay control; then added tenant fairness with Scorecard and edge-case detectors with regulators; then replaced CoDel with Cinnamon for priority-aware, auto-tuned, PID-controlled shedding; and finally unified local and remote signals into Cinnamon via BYOS to eliminate token-bucket split-brain and improve stability.</Person2>

<Person1>That’s the arc.

And the deeper insight is: overload management isn’t just “rate limiting.” It’s control theory plus distributed systems reality plus product priorities.

If you don’t incorporate priority, you’ll drop the wrong traffic.
If you don’t incorporate correct signals, you’ll shed too early or too late.
If you don’t unify control, you’ll get oscillations and inconsistent behavior.
And if you don’t fail fast, you’ll die by memory and tail latency.</Person1>

<Person2>One more question: why was concurrency such a key signal compared to QPS?</Person2>

<Person1>Because QPS doesn’t reflect cost variability. Concurrency naturally captures latency inflation. Under overload, latency rises, which increases in-flight operations for the same arrival rate. That’s often the earliest and most reliable indicator that the system is saturating.

And concurrency is also closer to resource usage: CPU threads, DB connections, locks, replication pipeline occupancy—all of those correlate with “how many things are happening at once.”</Person1>

<Person2>And then priority determines which of those things should be allowed to happen at once when you can’t do everything.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly.

So if you’re building something similar, the takeaway isn’t “use Cinnamon.” Most teams won’t. The takeaway is to build a unified, priority-aware admission control system close to the storage layer, driven by the right signals, and governed by a stable control loop rather than static thresholds.</Person1>

<Person2>And with a framework mindset—BYOS—so you can add new overload signals as your system evolves.</Person2>

<Person1>Yes. Because overload signals change as systems grow. Today it’s concurrency and memory. Tomorrow it’s replication lag, compaction debt, cache churn, or something you haven’t discovered yet.

A pluggable architecture means you can incorporate those signals without creating a new one-off limiter that fights with everything else.</Person1>

<Person2>That’s a great place to land. Overload is inevitable; cascading failure is optional—if you invest in intelligent, unified load management.</Person2>

<Person1>Exactly. That’s the journey Uber shared: from static, brittle rate limiting to a dynamic, priority-aware, signal-driven platform that keeps databases stable and fair under pressure.</Person1>
